message.content:
## Adversarial Claim Verifier (voter 2/3)

Be SKEPTICAL. Try to REFUTE this claim. ≥2/3 refutations kill it.

## Research question
National Life Group (NLG) / Life Insurance Company of the Southwest (LSW) TERM LIFE INSURANCE products — comprehensive product reference. Cover: (1) the full current term lineup and product names (e.g. the "G" guaranteed level-premium series Term 10-G / 15-G / 20-G / 30-G, form series ICC18-20522, and any other current term products like LSW Level Term / annually-renewable term); (2) issue ages and available level term lengths; (3) the level-premium period then annually-renewable-to-age-95 structure, whether the policy has cash value/dividends (it does not); (4) the conversion privilege — window (first 20 years or to age 70 if earlier), no evidence of insurability, equivalent rate class, what permanent products it converts to; (5) the Accelerated Benefit Rider (ABR) living-benefit suite available on term (Terminal, Chronic, Critical Illness, Critical Injury, Alzheimer's) — cost (no additional premium), triggers, discounted/condition-specific payout, and per-insured lifetime benefit limits and how they vary by state; (6) premium structure and rate classes (Elite/Preferred/Select/Standard/Express, tobacco/non-tobacco); (7) state availability variations; (8) foreign-national / non-US-resident rules for term (minimum face, US nexus/solicitation/delivery requirements, whether Brazil/"B" country is eligible, whether term is even offered to foreign nationals). Prioritize NLG/LSW primary sources: nationallife.com, the Life Product Quick Reference Chart, agent guides, product guides, rider brochures, and the Foreign National Guidelines. Distinguish guaranteed/structural facts from rate-sheet-driven current rates (which change often). Produce a cited report with source URLs.

## Claim under review
"NLG/LSW's current term lineup is branded 'Term LSW and Term NL Life' and offers level-term lengths of ART (annually renewable term), 10-year, 15-year, 20-year, and 30-year."

**Source:** https://pinneyinsurance.com/underwriting-docs/National%20Life%20Group-LSW%20Underwriting_Guide.pdf (primary)
**Supporting quote:** "Term LSW and Term NL Life ... ART 10 YR 15 YR 20 YR 30 YR"

## Checklist
1. Is the claim actually supported by the quote, or is it an overreach/misread?
2. WebSearch for contradicting evidence — does any credible source dispute or heavily qualify this?
3. Is the source quality sufficient for the claim's strength? (extraordinary claims need primary sources)
4. Is the claim outdated? (check dates — old claims about fast-moving fields are suspect)
5. Is this a marketing claim / press release / cherry-picked benchmark / forum speculation?

**refuted=true** if: unsupported by quote / contradicted / low-quality source for strong claim / outdated / marketing fluff.
**refuted=false** ONLY if: claim is well-supported, current, and source quality matches claim strength.
Default to refuted=true if uncertain.

Structured output only. Evidence MUST be specific.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- anthropic-skills:consolidate-memory: Reflective pass over your memory files — merge duplicates, fix stale facts, prune the index.
- anthropic-skills:docx: Use this skill whenever the user wants to create, read, edit, or manipulate Word documents (.docx files) or Word templates (.dotx files). Triggers include: any mention of 'Word doc', 'word document', '.docx', '.dotx', or requests to produce professional documents with formatting like tables of contents, headings, page numbers, or letterheads. Also use when extracting or reorganizing content from .docx or .dotx files, inserting or replacing images in documents, performing find-and-replace in Word files, working with tracked changes or comments, or converting content into a polished Word document. If the user asks for a 'report', 'memo', 'letter', 'template', or similar deliverable as a Word or .docx file, use this skill. Do NOT use for PDFs, spreadsheets, Google Docs, or general coding tasks unrelated to document generation.
- anthropic-skills:morning: Render the user's morning brief as a styled HTML artifact, or set it up as a recurring weekday task. Use when: morning brief, daily brief, set up my morning brief, what's my day look like, run my brief.
- anthropic-skills:pdf: Use this skill whenever the user wants to do anything with PDF files. This includes reading or extracting text/tables from PDFs, combining or merging multiple PDFs into one, splitting PDFs apart, rotating pages, adding watermarks, creating new PDFs, filling PDF forms, encrypting/decrypting PDFs, extracting images, and OCR on scanned PDFs to make them searchable. If the user mentions a .pdf file or asks to produce one, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:pptx: Use this skill any time a .pptx or .potx file is involved in any way — as input, output, or both. This includes: creating slide decks, pitch decks, or presentations; reading, parsing, or extracting text from any .pptx or .potx file (even if the extracted content will be used elsewhere, like in an email or summary); editing, modifying, or updating existing presentations; combining or splitting slide files; working with templates (.potx), layouts, speaker notes, or comments. Trigger whenever the user mentions "deck," "slides," "presentation," or references a .pptx or .potx filename, regardless of what they plan to do with the content afterward. If a .pptx or .potx file needs to be opened, created, or touched, use this skill.
- anthropic-skills:schedule: Create or update a scheduled task that runs automatically. Use when the user says things like "every day", "each morning", "remind me in an hour", "run this at noon", or wants to reschedule an existing task.
- anthropic-skills:setup-cowork: Guided Cowork setup — install role-matched plugins, connect your tools, try a skill.
- anthropic-skills:skill-creator: Create new skills, modify and improve existing skills, and measure skill performance. Use when users want to create a skill from scratch, edit, or optimize an existing skill, run evals to test a skill, benchmark skill performance with variance analysis, or optimize a skill's description for better triggering accuracy.
- anthropic-skills:xlsx: Use this skill any time a spreadsheet file is the primary input or output. This means any task where the user wants to: open, read, edit, or fix an existing .xlsx, .xlsm, .xltx, .csv, or .tsv file (e.g., adding columns, computing formulas, formatting, charting, cleaning messy data); create a new spreadsheet from scratch or from other data sources; or convert between tabular file formats. Trigger especially when the user references a spreadsheet file by name or path — even casually (like "the xlsx in my downloads") — and wants something done to it or produced from it. Also trigger for cleaning or restructuring messy tabular data files (malformed rows, misplaced headers, junk data) into proper spreadsheets. The deliverable must be a spreadsheet file. Do NOT trigger when the primary deliverable is a Word document, HTML report, standalone Python script, database pipeline, or Google Sheets API integration, even if tabular data is involved.
- dataviz: Use this skill whenever you are about to create ANY chart, graph, plot, dashboard, or data visualization, in ANY output medium — an HTML or React artifact, inline SVG, plotting code in any library (matplotlib, plotly, d3, Recharts, …), an image/PNG you will render and upload, or a chart shared into Slack. Read it BEFORE writing the first line of chart code, choosing chart colors, building a stat tile / meter / KPI row, or laying out a dashboard. Produces visualizations that read as one system — elegant, accessible, consistent in light and dark — using a brand-neutral placeholder palette you swap for your own. Teaches a design-system-agnostic method: a form heuristic, a color formula with a runnable validator, mark specs, and interaction rules. A validated default palette is documented in `references/palette.md` — swap that file's values for your brand's. Triggers on: "chart", "graph", "plot", "data viz", "visualization", "dashboard", "analytics", "visualize data", "categorical colors", "sequential / diverging palette", "stat tile", "sparkline", "heatmap", "legend", "axis", "tooltip", "chart colors", "color by series".
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- artifact-capabilities: Runtime capabilities a published Artifact can declare — calling the user's claude.ai connectors (MCP) from the page, and future abilities. Load this BEFORE passing `capabilities` to the Artifact tool or writing any `window.claude.mcp` code.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by exercising it end-to-end and observing behavior — drive the affected flow, not just tests or typecheck. Run before committing nontrivial changes; bootstraps this repo's project verify skill if none exists yet. Don't invoke it on a diff that only touches tests, docs, or other code with no runtime surface to drive (a change to product source always has one) — there's nothing to observe.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

## Search Results: National Life Group LSW Term Life Insurance Products

I found comprehensive information about National Life Group's LSW Level Term life insurance products. Here's what the search revealed:

### LSW Level Term Products

National Life Group offers the following LSW Level Term products:
- **10-G**: Offers premiums that are guaranteed and level for 10 years
- **15-G**: Offers premiums that are guaranteed and level for 15 years  
- **20-G**: Has guaranteed level premiums for 20 years
- **30-G**: Has guaranteed level premiums for 30 years

### Coverage Details

Coverage under the 15-G and 20-G plans extends to age 95. 

The products also include 15-NG plans that offer premiums that are guaranteed and level for 10 years, and current level for 15 years, and similar non-guaranteed variants for the 20 and 30-year options.

### Optional Features

All term plans offer optional Accelerated Benefits Riders, as well as the Children's Term Rider and the Waiver of Premium Rider. All riders are available on an insured spouse's policy except for the Children's Term Rider, and riders are optional and available at an additional cost unless otherwise noted, though riders may not be available in all states.

The search results included product guides and underwriting documentation that provide detailed specifications for these LSW term life insurance offerings.


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

I've found multiple National Life Group underwriting guides and documents related to LSW (Life Insurance Company of the Southwest) and National Life Life (NL) products, including information about their term life insurance offerings and ART (Annual Renewable Term) products.

Based on the search results, here's what I found:

Annual Renewable Term (ART) is a competitively priced, annually renewable term life insurance policy with guaranteed premiums that increase annually. The search results reference multiple underwriting guides from National Life Group that cover both National Life Insurance Company (NL) and LSW products.

The documents I found include:

1. **National Life Group Underwriting Guide** (2023 version) - covering comprehensive underwriting requirements
2. **LSW Underwriting Guide** - specific to Life Insurance Company of the Southwest products
3. **Term Product Guides** - detailing ART and other term offerings with issue ages and face amount limits

EZ Underwriting is available for ages 18-50 up to $2M, ages 51-60 up to $1M, and ages 61-65 up to $250,000, with a minimum face amount of $100,000.

The documents cover underwriting programs, medical testing requirements, financial underwriting, and other guidelines for both NL and LSW term and permanent life insurance products.

Would you like me to search for more specific information about particular underwriting criteria, face amount limits, or other specific topics from these guides?


REMINDER: You MUST include the sources above in your response to the user using markdown hyperlinks.

Now I need to verify the exact phrasing by checking the actual LSW underwriting guide text. The challenge is that these are image-based PDFs, so I'll try fetching the LSW Term product guide from the available sources to confirm the specific terminology used.

content.content:
I was unable to locate a term products table with headers or product names "Term LSW" and "Term NL Life" in the provided PDF content. Looking at the quote from the underwriting guide, I can see those term lengths are listed, but I'm realizing "Term LSW" and "Term NL Life" aren't actually product brand names at all. Given the skeptical standard here, that branding overreach could be enough to weaken the overall claim, though the source itself is legitimate. The product names do appear in National Life Group's reference materials as "Term (LSW)" and "Term (NL Life)" split by carrier, so the claim isn't fabricated. The term lengths and structure are accurate—the only semantic stretch is calling these carrier designations a "brand," but the underlying facts about the two carriers offering term products in those specific lengths hold up. The actual retail branding is "LSW Level Term" with the guaranteed-level series (10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G), whereas the claim labels them as "Term LSW" and "Term NL Life"—which are really just underwriting-guide groupings by issuer, not the product brand itself. So while the term lengths listed are accurate and well-sourced, the branding identification is an overreach that misreads the table headers. Given that the checklist flags this kind of misidentification as grounds for refutation, I'm leaning toward marking this as refuted despite the core facts being correct. Based on the search results, I found information about National Life Group's LSW Level Term products:

## LSW Level Term Product Lineup

National Life Group offers LSW Level Term products in the following variations: LSW Level Term 10-G (10-year level premium policy), LSW Level Term 15-G (premiums guaranteed and level for 15 years), LSW Level Term 20-G (premiums guaranteed and level for 20 years), and LSW Level Term 30-G (premiums guaranteed and level for 30 years).

There are also LSW Level Term 30-NG plans available, where the 30-G version has guaranteed level premiums for 30 years, while the 30-NG plan has current level premiums for 30 years with guarantees for the first 10 years.

These LSW Level Term 10, 15, 20, and 30 products (both G and NG plans) are low-cost, convertible term life insurance policies with level premium periods and level death benefits.

Coverage under LSW Level Term plans extends to age 95.

The "G" designation indicates guaranteed level premiums throughout the specified term period, while "NG" indicates non-guaranteed rates (current rates for a specified period with a guaranteed period for the first 10 years).

content.thinking:
I'm seeing the product structure now — the consumer-facing "LSW Level Term" brand comes in G-series variants (10-G through 30-G) plus NG options, while the underwriting guide separates products by their issuing carriers, with "Term LSW" from Life Insurance Company of the Southwest and "Term NL Life" from National Life Insurance Company.

The claim conflates these carrier-grouping labels from the underwriting guide with the actual consumer brand, which is a misreading of how the product reference chart is organized. The term lengths (ART plus 10, 15, 20, 30 years) are well-documented across sources, but the branding part of the claim doesn't hold up. Confidence is the real question here — the term lengths are factually correct, but calling those carrier-grouping labels a "brand" is a mischaracterization. The actual retail brand is "LSW Level Term." I'll mark this as refuted with medium confidence, since the branding claim is genuinely misleading even if the underlying product details are accurate.

content.text:
I have enough evidence to render a skeptical verdict.

The key finding: multiple NLG/LSW product guides consistently show the actual consumer/retail brand name for the current term line is "LSW Level Term," sold as the guaranteed "G" series (10-G, 15-G, 20-G, 30-G) plus NG variants, all extending to age 95. The phrase "Term LSW and Term NL Life" is not a product brand — it is how the underwriting guide / Life Product Quick Reference chart *groups the term line by issuing carrier* (LSW = Life Insurance Company of the Southwest; NL Life = National Life Insurance Company, the New York carrier). The claim misreads those carrier-grouping table labels as the lineup's brand name.

The term-length component (ART, 10, 15, 20, 30) is well corroborated by multiple sources.

input.evidence: The claim conflates underwriting-guide carrier-grouping labels with a product brand — an overreach/misread (checklist #1). The primary Pinney PDF is titled "Products issued by National Life Insurance Company" and, like the NLG Life Product Quick Reference chart, splits the term line by its two issuing carriers: "Term LSW" (Life Insurance Company of the Southwest) and "Term NL Life" (National Life Insurance Company, the NY carrier). These are carrier categories, not a brand. Multiple NLG/LSW product guides (theinsgroup.net LSW Level Term Product Guide; labonteinsurance.net "LSW Level Term 20-G" illustration; totalfinancial LSW UW guide) consistently show the actual current retail brand is "LSW Level Term," sold as the guaranteed G-series 10-G/15-G/20-G/30-G (plus NG variants), coverage to age 95. So the lineup is NOT "branded 'Term LSW and Term NL Life'"; its brand is LSW Level Term. Note the term-LENGTH portion of the claim (ART + 10/15/20/30) IS supported: NLG offers ART plus 10/15/20/30-year level term (confirmed across ogletreefinancial review and product guides). Caveat: the cited PDFs were not machine-text-extractable (binary/image streams), so the exact quote string could not be directly re-read; verdict rests on corroborating primary product guides that uniformly brand the product "LSW Level Term," contradicting the "branded 'Term LSW and Term NL Life'" assertion.
